Vaylix Docs
Vaylix on GitHub
Vaylix Docs Transport-first database / Rust workspace

Technical documentation for operating and understanding Vaylix.

Use this documentation to build, run, operate, and understand Vaylix. It covers the current system as implemented today: a single-node Rust key/value database with a transport-first architecture, durable persistence, and clearly defined operational boundaries.

Get started Read the project context GitHub
Deployment profile
Repository
vaylix/vaylix
Primary runtime
Tokio multi-client server
Storage model
WAL + snapshot recovery
Transport
Framed binary over TCP/TLS
Request identity
UUID-based request IDs

Quick start

Read this next

  1. Why Vaylix Scope, intent, and design posture.
  2. Run server and client Local flow, credentials, and URL usage.
  3. CLI reference All current flags and command-line semantics.
  4. System design Layering, engine isolation, and worker model.

Documentation map

Install and build

Compile the Rust workspace, understand the crate layout, and get the docs aligned with the codebase.

Run a local node

Start the server, connect with the client, and verify auth, TLS, and the transport boundary.

Inspect the wire protocol

Review framed requests, UUID request IDs, structured errors, compression flags, and response semantics.

Understand persistence

Learn snapshot flow, WAL replay, encrypted-at-rest storage, recovery ordering, and rotation behavior.

Review the security model

Authentication, optional TLS, audit logging, and current operational boundaries are documented here.

Read the architecture

See the transport-first layering and the constraints that keep engine, server, and client concerns separated.

Current implementation

  • Single-node key/value engine with a string-to-string model
  • Transport shared by client and server, with framed binary messages
  • Tokio-based multi-client server with authenticated sessions
  • WAL plus snapshot durability, storage keyring management, and audit logging

Boundaries to keep in mind

  • Replication and sharding are roadmap items, not implemented features
  • Transactions are atomic on a single node, not distributed ACID
  • TLS is available but not mandatory by default
  • Operational guidance should stay aligned with the maintained project context page
